reasoning: A is the most rigorous: it separates the descriptive from the prescriptive question, lists five rival explanations including the non-obvious reverse-confounding-via-health pathway, states that reverse causation alone explains the entire 20% gap, nails the relative-vs-absolute and large-n-inflates-p points, flags the supplement as a 'semi-attached figure,' and gives a concrete calibrated probability (10-15%) plus a Mendelian-randomization falsifier. B matches A's structure and the 'swap of subject' framing but offers slightly less mechanistic differentiation and no numeric confidence. C is well-organized and adds useful real-literature context but is less crisp on the statistics and gives no probability. D covers the same ground more diffusely, weights biological plausibility more heavily, and lands softer ('there may be a real modest association'), making it the least discriminating handling of causation.
